title: Creflo Dollar's $65 Million Prayer: Pastor Asks Flock to Buy Him a Third Jet
teaser: Prosperity gospel pastor Creflo Dollar launched a campaign in March 2015 asking supporters to collectively donate $65 million for a Gulfstream G650 private jet. Dollar was already operating two Gulfstream jets at the time and simultaneously preaching that giving to his ministry was the key to personal financial blessing.
body:
There is a particular species of theological argument that defies easy summarization. In March 2015, Creflo Dollar, senior pastor of World Changers Church International in College Park, Georgia, formally asked his congregation and online followers to donate $65 million toward the purchase of a Gulfstream G650 private jet. The request was not a rumor, a misquote, or a satirical invention. It was a real, organized fundraising campaign with a real price tag attached to a real aircraft model. The number $65 million deserves a moment of quiet reflection before we proceed.

Dollar's stated rationale for the acquisition was that the jet would support his global ministry operations. This is, in the abstract, a coherent argument for any organization conducting international travel. The complicating detail, reported at the time, is that Dollar was already operating not one but two Gulfstream jets when he made this request. The proposal was not to replace an aging aircraft or consolidate a fleet. It was, by any reasonable accounting, an expansion of an already substantial private aviation portfolio, funded entirely by his congregation and online donors.

World Changers Church International is among the most prominent prosperity gospel institutions in the United States. The prosperity gospel teaches, in its core formulation, that financial giving to a ministry is a mechanism of divine return: donors give, and God multiplies the gift back to them. Dollar has built a substantial media operation and international ministry profile on this doctrinal foundation. It is a framework that, when examined alongside a $65 million jet campaign, generates a certain unavoidable irony.

The irony is structural, not incidental. Dollar was simultaneously teaching his congregation that their path to personal financial blessing ran directly through donations to his ministry, while asking that same congregation to collectively fund an aircraft that would cost more than most American neighborhoods are worth. The beneficiary of the financial giving, in this specific transaction, was Creflo Dollar's ministry. The promised beneficiary of the spiritual giving, per the theology, was the congregation. The math of who was receiving the Gulfstream is not ambiguous.

The Gulfstream G650 is not a utilitarian aircraft. It is widely regarded as one of the most luxurious and expensive private jets available on the commercial market. A ministry organization seeking cost-effective international travel has numerous alternatives at substantially lower price points. The selection of the G650 as the specific target of this campaign is itself a data point that serious observers were not inclined to overlook.

Dollar framed the jet as a tool of ministry, a vessel for spreading the gospel across international distances. It is worth noting that commercial aviation exists, that charter services exist, and that many international religious organizations conduct global outreach without maintaining fleets of ultra-long-range private jets. These are not novel observations. They were made by critics almost immediately after the campaign became public, and they were made with some force.

The public response to the campaign was swift and, to put it diplomatically, not uniformly supportive. The fundraising effort attracted significant national media coverage and drew criticism from religious commentators, journalists, and ordinary observers across the ideological spectrum. The spectacle of a televangelist asking his followers to collectively spend $65 million on a luxury aircraft while preaching about the blessings of financial giving proved to be a compelling news story. Dollar and his organization eventually took the fundraising campaign down from public-facing platforms following the backlash, though the underlying ambition was not abandoned.

It is worth pausing on the organizational context. World Changers Church International is a large and well-resourced organization. Dollar's ministry has generated substantial revenue over the course of its operation, supported by television broadcasting, speaking engagements, book sales, and regular congregational giving. That resource base makes the decision to solicit $65 million in small donations from followers for a single aircraft purchase a choice, not a necessity.

The campaign's structure deserves specific attention. Dollar did not approach a bank, a wealthy donor, or an institutional lender. He asked individuals, including people of modest means who believed that giving to his ministry would improve their own financial circumstances, to collectively fund a jet. The fundraising model placed the burden of a $65 million luxury purchase on the shoulders of an audience that the prosperity gospel had specifically conditioned to view giving as a spiritual obligation with personal financial returns. That is a closed loop worth mapping carefully.

Dollar is not a marginal figure in American religious broadcasting. He has built a recognizable national profile over several decades, and his congregation has supported a significant institutional operation. The jet campaign did not emerge from nowhere; it emerged from an environment in which large financial requests to congregants had become routine enough that a $65 million ask could be presented as a straightforward ministry need. The public reaction suggested that, at least on this occasion, the broader audience had a different assessment of what constituted a reasonable request.

Accountability journalism has an obligation to state clearly what happened, without embellishment. What happened is this: a pastor who preaches that giving money to his ministry produces divine financial returns asked his congregation to give him $65 million so he could own a third private jet. He was already operating two private jets. The jet in question was among the most expensive in the world. These facts do not require editorial amplification. They are self-completing.

The incident stands as a documented case study in the internal tensions of prosperity gospel theology when applied at scale. The doctrine teaches that the faithful give and God returns the blessing to them. In the specific transaction Dollar proposed, the congregation would give $65 million and Creflo Dollar would receive a Gulfstream G650. Whether that sequence satisfies the theological promise of the prosperity gospel is a question Dollar's congregation was left to answer for themselves, presumably from seats that were not located in a ultra-long-range private aircraft.
subjectTitle: Senior Pastor, World Changers Church International
